title: Vivo X300 vs iPhone 17 Pro vs Xiaomi 17 Pro: The BEST Compact Phone of 2025?
transcript: Can thin and light truly coexist with camera capabilities in small screen flagships? We're testing three top contenders. The Vivo X300, Xiaomi 17 Pro, and iPhone 17 Pro. Let's find out which is the true all-around camera king by looking at their design, photos, and video. Since they are all small screen flagships, the dimensions of these three phones are actually not that different. They all have a 6.3 in flat screen design. But in terms of weight and thickness control, you can see a clear difference. The Vivo X300 weighs 190 g and is 7.95 mm thick, making it the thinnest and lightest of the three. The Xiaomi 17 Pro weighs 192 g and 8 mm thick, while the iPhone 17 Pro reaches 204 g and 8.75 mm thick. The iPhone as a whole is heavier [music] and thicker. In terms of bezel control, the Vivo X300 uses a 1.05 mm ultra narrow symmetrical bezel design. The front view is very uniform and minimalist. The Xiaomi 17 Pros control is [music] also good close to the X300. In comparison, the black borders on this iPhone 17 Pro [music] are slightly wider and visually it doesn't achieve the same immersive feeling as the other two. Let's take a closer look at the hand feel. The Vivo X300 has a smooth transition between the back glass and the camera module. Paired with the silk-like AG glass back and the micro curved middle frame, it fits the palm of your hand, and one-handed operation is very comfortable. In particular, the contact between your finger and the camera area is more natural. The Xiaomi 17 Pro uses a metal middle frame and a fiber back cover. The body is quite rounded, but unfortunately, the feel of the fiber is very average. The iPhone 17 Pro uses a uni-body aluminum alloy frame that extends to the back. The glass is only in the MagSafe area. The hand feel is still okay. When holding it, it's similar to the Xiaomi 17 Pro. Because the square camera module occupies a large area, the index finger easily touches the corner, which slightly affects the hand feel. Putting the three phones together and comparing the front and back visual experience and the actual inhand feel, this Vivo X300 is indeed the one with the best hand feel and the best fit. Moving into the camera section, let's first look at the camera parameters of the three phones. The Vivo X300 is equipped with a triple camera system including a 200 megapixel large sensor HPB, a 50 megapixel Zeiss ultra wide angle and a 50 megapixel Zeiss APO super telephoto lens. The Xiaomi 17 Pro uses a combination of 350 megapixel lenses. The main is the Light Hunter 950L, the ultrawide is the OV50M, and the telephoto is the JN5. The iPhone 17 Pro still uses three 48 megapixel fusion pixel lenses. The main, wide, and telephoto are IMX903, IMX982, and IMX973, respectively. Entering the actual shooting session, we'll start with a few groups of typical shooting scenarios. First is the main camera shooting outdoors during the day. All three phones can output clear [music] images in well-lit conditions. The overall color styles are different. Vivo leans towards neutral and transparent. Xiaomi's colors are rich and visually pleasing. The iPhone is more natural and has high tolerance. The Vivo X300 also provides various style options. Especially if you don't like excessive AI intervention, you can turn on natural light and shadow during shooting. This way, the effect is closer to that of a camera. The Vivo X300's 200 megapixels also provide more creative possibilities. Through the oneshot multiple crops function, you can freely crop to different perspectives after taking a picture. The image quality is still clear and the creative freedom is greater. The third group is shooting distant [music] buildings, testing the resolution of the telephoto lens. Because the native focal lengths of these three products are different, Vivo is 70 mm, Xiaomi is [music] 115 mm, and iPhone is 100 mm, we can only control the proportion of the subject in the frame to be about the same. Let's look at the sample images directly. The Xiaomi 17 Pro and Vivo X300, whether zooming in to see details or the overall look and color performance of the image, the two phones are very close. Only the iPhone 17 Pro's resolution is slightly worse. After zooming in, you can still see a lot of noise. Some people may be worried that the telephoto lens's magnification isn't enough for very distant objects. [music] Actually, this time the Vivo X300 also supports a 2.35x telephoto converter lens. You can get amazing shots even from a distance. In terms of video shooting experience, the three phones each have their own focus. The Vivo X300 supports 4K video recording across all focal lengths, covering 4K 60fps, cinematic portrait, 4K 120 fps slow motion, and 10- bit log recording. The Xiaomi 17 Pro also supports 4K 120 fps recording, but some filter modes only support 1080p 30 fps. The iPhone 17 Pro supports full focal length ProRes log video recording and 4K 120 fps shooting. Based solely on the log video experience, Vivo and iPhone are clearly much stronger than Xiaomi. But considering that most users don't spend too much time on color grading, direct out of camera video is the scenario for the vast majority of people. In that case, it comes down to whose skin tone tuning, image quality, and stabilization are better. It's very clear. If you want direct output, choose the Vivo X300. If you want to do secondary color grading, choose the iPhone 17 Pro. To summarize, the Vivo X300 presents the most well-rounded and balanced overall quality across the three main aspects: design and handfield, photo output, and video experience. The body is light but not flimsy, thin, but not fragile. The 1.05 mm symmetrical bezels provide a purer front view. The 200 megapixel main camera transforms resolution into creativity. Full focal length 4K recording is equipped with sea 4.5 level stabilization, keeping the frame steady even while walking. Truly achieving shoot and get a masterpiece. The Xiaomi 17 Pro is more expressive in stylized camera [music] and backup camera features. The iPhone 17 Pro wins with its stable and reliable workflow and ecosystem integration, which is suitable for professional users who already own Apple devices. But when we set the test for a small screen camera flagship, it needs [music] to be light and thin without sacrificing handfield. The direct output needs to look good and be stable and easy to use. You need to be able to shoot as soon as you pick it up and post it right after shooting. The answer is already clear. The Vivo X300 is more like the phone you'd be willing to take out every day. The one that gives you the creative impulse as soon as you hold it. If you are looking for a phone with complete [music] camera specs, reliable photo output, and is sufficiently light and nimble, I am more willing to cast this vote to the Vivo X300.

title: I Put the iPhone 17 Pro Under a Microscope (And Found This...)
transcript: You are looking inside a brand-new,  factory-sealed, iPhone 17 Pro telephoto   lens. And yes… it is full of dust. Apple won’t  replace these, because this level of dust is   considered normal. But the dust… as shocking as it  is… isn't even the most incredible thing I found.   The print quality on the packaging  is, itself, a technical feat.   It’s a perfect dot-matrix of pixels. The seals are intact. This is   100% a new, unopened product. But look at the paper corner of the   box. The precision of that fold is the first sign  of manufacturing tolerances we should expect. It’s   impressive. You can feel the dense texture of this  paper shimmering as the camera shifts angles. When we tear that seal, you can see the two-layer  paper. Why? Who knows. But it's a clue... that   nothing in this box is as simple as it looks. And here's the phone. Right away, magnification   shows a gap between the white plastic back and  the aluminum unibody. It's wider than I expected. But then, we move to the antenna line around  the camera plateau, and the opposite is true.   The plastic is perfectly molded to the metal.  Zero gaps. This level of precision is likely   a "mold-in-place" process. Impressive. The anodized coating on the plateau shifts   from matte to reflective depending on the  angle — an interesting optical property. But let’s get to the main event. The ultrawide  lens is the reason I bought this phone. I   plan to test its macro capabilities next. It’s  coated so dark, it hides everything behind it. Only with added light do the internal details  appear — along with more dust speckles.   We're talking about the inside dust here. The  outside particles? They're on me, of course. Now, the main camera. And it’s the same  story. A whole cloud of micro-dust particles,   floating at different depths, reminds me of a  constellation of stars in the sky. So beautiful! The flash isn't just an LED; it's a complex  diffuser with a conical ripple effect. The rear microphone. It's not just a hole.  It’s a crater. And the chamfered edge has   a honeycomb-like structure. We can even see the  circular tool marks from the chamfering bit. This   means it was drilled after the anodization. The LiDAR scanner? An absolute black void.   No light penetrates it. The Action Button... more dust. This could   be from my environment. I can't blame the factory  for this one. Still, I haven’t touched it yet,   mind you. The new camera button... same thing.  But the build is interesting. It looks like a   glass inlay with a stainless steel border. The bottom components... The speaker grates   are clean—for now. This is the last  time they'll ever look this nice. The screw... it's a Pentalobe, of course, but  look at the surface. Circular waves, like a   water ripple. The USB-C port... a mesmerizing,  perfect fit of steel, plastic, and aluminum,   laser-engraved with its part number inside. Turning to the front, the speaker gap between   the screen and the aluminum housing hides  a protective plastic insert with holes.   With enough light, we see the front camera...  and yes, you guessed it, another dust cloud. But that's not the story. The real story is  the display itself. Look... at the pixels. This is the ProMotion XDR display, at  the sub-pixel level. This is not art;   this is micro-engineering on an insane scale. You are looking at the individual Red, Green,   and Blue sub-pixels. The blue element is the  largest square. The red is smaller. The greens   are rectangles. Each one is secured by what  appears to be gold-plated copper contacts. And the entire array is overlaid  with a separate grid... that is   almost certainly the touch sensor. Look at the edge of the Dynamic Island   cutout. They have perfectly terminated the  pixel array at the edge. No wasted components! From dust trapped in the lenses to microscopic  engravings in the USB‑C port, the iPhone 17 Pro   is both a showcase of engineering and a  reminder that perfection doesn’t exist.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max Not Ringing for Incoming Calls? Here’s the Fix!
transcript: Is your iPhone 17 Pro or Pro Max not ringing when someone calls or you're not receiving any incoming calls at all? Here are some simple steps that will help you fix this in under minutes. First of all, make sure that your iPhone is not in silent mode, which you can toggle off by long pressing the action button and making sure it says ring. Also, just open the control center and toggle it off and make sure do not disturb or any other focus mode isn't enabled. After turning this off, if you're still not getting calls, then let's simply force restart our iPhone, which may fix it. Just press the volume up, volume down, then hold down the side button and keep holding it until the Apple logo appears. Make sure you do this quickly. The second most common reason why your phone may not be ringing is attentionaware features. So let's try turning it off by opening settings. Scroll down. Go to Face ID and passcode. Then go down and disable attention aware features. As you can see here, it can lower the volume if it thinks that you're looking at your phone. Now your iPhone should ring properly. To make sure your ringtone is set to max, just open settings, then scroll down, find sound and haptics, and slide the ringtone and alert tone to the max. You can also make sure that haptics is set to always play. If your iPhone still won't ring, then this can also be caused by your content and privacy restrictions. To check that, open settings once again, and this time go to screen time. and communication limits. And make sure both of these are set to everyone. If you're still not receiving calls from some specific people or no one at all, then from the settings app, scroll down, go to apps, and go to the phone app. From here, scroll down and make sure you're not screening any unknown callers. So just select never. Then also disable call filtering so that your phone still rings if the number is not saved. And lastly check whether you have blocked any caller by mistake. If the problem persists then there are some accessibility settings that may be causing this. So this time from the settings go to accessibility then touch scroll down select call audio routing. First of all make sure it's set to automatic so that it doesn't go to any Bluetooth devices and also double check that autoans answer calls is disabled. Now your iPhone will ring for every call that you get so you won't miss anything. Hope you found this helpful and stay tuned for

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max Date and Time Settings | How to Adjust Easily!
transcript: So, you want to change the time on your iPhone 17 or 17 Pro Max. So, here's how to manually set the date and time and easily adjust your time widget on the home or lock screen. So, first you have to head over to your settings and from general choose date and time. So, if you see that the time cannot be changed, it means that automatically is turn on. So, just toggle off this set automatically. Now you will get the option to manually set the date and time just the way you want. So now if you want to change the time shown on your widget, just tap and hold the widget and now go for edit widget. Choose your preferred location. So the widget shows the correct time for the place. And that's it. You have now updated the time on your iPhone 17 both manually and for widgets. I hope this guide helped you a lot. Thanks for watching.
